evals.prompt: hey, I need BDD tests for my Button component. it has variants (primary, secondary, danger), can be disabled, and shows a loading spinner when isLoading is true. the component is at src/components/Button/Button.tsx
evals.expected_output: A .feature file with scenarios covering each variant, disabled state, and loading state, plus a .steps.tsx file implementing those scenarios with RTL. Tests should pass.
assertions: A file src/components/Button/Button.feature is created
A file src/components/Button/Button.steps.tsx is created
The .feature file contains at least 3 distinct Scenario blocks
The .feature file uses parameterized steps with quoted strings for variants (e.g. "primary", "secondary")
The .feature file does NOT contain any CSS selectors, data-testid attributes, or class names
The .steps.tsx file imports from @testing-library/react/pure and @amiceli/vitest-cucumber
The .steps.tsx file imports @testing-library/jest-dom/vitest for semantic matchers
The .steps.tsx file uses loadFeature with the correct path to the .feature file
The .steps.tsx file uses describeFeature, not describe or it
The .steps.tsx file uses AfterEachScenario with cleanup(), not beforeEach
All parameterized step callbacks use _ctx as first argument
Step matchers use {string} or {int} templates, not regex
The .steps.tsx file does NOT use .toBeTruthy() or .toBeDefined() for element presence checks
The .steps.tsx file uses toBeInTheDocument() for verifying element presence
The .steps.tsx file uses toHaveAttribute() for attribute assertions (not getAttribute())
The .steps.tsx file includes at least one interaction scenario using userEvent (click, keyboard, etc.)
npm test passes for the Button component

evals.prompt: I already have BDD tests for my Modal component but I need to add scenarios for: 1) closing the modal when pressing Escape, 2) trapping focus inside the modal. The existing files are at src/components/Modal/Modal.feature and src/components/Modal/Modal.steps.tsx
evals.expected_output: The existing .feature and .steps.tsx files are updated with new scenarios appended, without overwriting existing scenarios. Tests pass.
assertions: The skill reads the existing Modal.feature before modifying it
The skill reads the existing Modal.steps.tsx before modifying it
Previously existing scenarios in Modal.feature are preserved unchanged
Two new Scenario blocks are added to Modal.feature (Escape key and focus trap)
Corresponding step definitions are added to Modal.steps.tsx
The new steps follow the same code style as existing steps
The new step definitions use userEvent for keyboard interactions (Escape key, Tab for focus trap)
The new step definitions do NOT use .toBeTruthy() for element checks — use toBeInTheDocument() or toHaveFocus()
npm test passes for the Modal component

evals.prompt: create bdd tests for the SearchInput component at src/components/SearchInput/SearchInput.tsx — it renders an input, calls onSearch when you type and press Enter, and shows a clear button when there's text. I think we already have some shared steps for input interactions
evals.expected_output: A .feature and .steps.tsx that reuse shared steps where applicable instead of duplicating them. The skill should check sharedSteps.ts and existing feature files first.
assertions: The skill reads src/test/sharedSteps.ts before writing step definitions
The skill checks existing .feature files for reusable step patterns
The .feature file is created at src/components/SearchInput/SearchInput.feature
The .steps.tsx file imports and uses shared steps where matching patterns exist
The .steps.tsx file imports @testing-library/jest-dom/vitest
The .feature file contains scenarios for: rendering, typing + Enter, and clear button
No step definition duplicates functionality already available in sharedSteps.ts
The scenario-to-requirement mapping is presented to the user before writing files
The .steps.tsx file uses userEvent for typing and keyboard interactions, not fireEvent
The .steps.tsx file does NOT use .toBeTruthy() for element presence checks

evals.prompt: I need BDD tests for my GamesList component at src/components/GamesList/GamesList.tsx — it imports an array of games from a data file and renders a card for each one. Each card shows the game title, a subtitle, and links to an external URL. The card opens in a new tab.
evals.expected_output: A .feature file with scenarios for rendering all games, verifying links, and new-tab behavior. The .steps.tsx must guard against empty data arrays and verify correct item count.
assertions: A file src/components/GamesList/GamesList.feature is created
A file src/components/GamesList/GamesList.steps.tsx is created
The .steps.tsx file asserts the data array is non-empty before iterating (expect(items.length).toBeGreaterThan(0) or equivalent)
The .steps.tsx file asserts the correct number of rendered items (e.g. getAllByRole('link')).toHaveLength(...))
The .steps.tsx file uses toBeInTheDocument() for presence checks, NOT .toBeTruthy()
The .steps.tsx file uses toHaveAttribute('href', ...) for link URL checks
The .steps.tsx file uses toHaveAttribute('target', '_blank') for new-tab checks
The .steps.tsx file uses toHaveAttribute('rel', expect.stringContaining('noopener')) for security checks
The .steps.tsx file imports @testing-library/jest-dom/vitest
npm test passes for the GamesList component

evals.prompt: create BDD tests for the UserProfile component at src/components/UserProfile/UserProfile.tsx
evals.expected_output: The skill attempts to read the component source, discovers it does not exist, and stops to ask the user instead of guessing or generating tests for a nonexistent component.
assertions: The skill attempts to read src/components/UserProfile/UserProfile.tsx
The skill detects the component file does not exist
The skill does NOT create a .feature or .steps.tsx file
The skill informs the user that the component was not found
The skill asks the user to provide the correct path or confirm the component exists

evals.prompt: add BDD tests for my Header component at src/components/Header/Header.tsx — this is a fresh project, I haven't installed any testing dependencies yet
evals.expected_output: The skill checks package.json, discovers missing dependencies, and informs the user before proceeding.
assertions: The skill checks package.json for @amiceli/vitest-cucumber
The skill checks package.json for @testing-library/react
The skill detects one or more missing dependencies
The skill informs the user which packages are missing
The skill offers to install the missing packages before proceeding
The skill does NOT scaffold test files before dependencies are resolved
